question: Which of the following is written in PascalCase and could be used as a React component name?

A.text: userProfile
A.feedback: This is camelCase, not PascalCase. React component names should start with a capital letter.

B.feedback: Correct! React component names are typically written in PascalCase.

C.text: user-profile
C.feedback: This is kebab-case, not PascalCase.

D.text: USERPROFILE
D.feedback: This is uppercase, not PascalCase.

question: Why are React component names usually written in PascalCase?

A.text: Because React uses capitalized names to identify components
A.feedback: Correct! React treats capitalized names as custom components and lowercase names as HTML elements.

B.text: Because JavaScript requires functions to start with capital letters
B.feedback: JavaScript does not require functions to start with capital letters.

C.text: Because PascalCase makes components render faster
C.feedback: PascalCase is for naming conventions and React recognition, not performance.

D.text: Because JSX only works with uppercase text
D.feedback: JSX works regardless of capitalization. React uses capitalization to distinguish components from HTML elements.

question: Which component name follows React naming conventions?

A.text: shoppingcart
A.feedback: This is lowercase and does not follow PascalCase naming conventions.

B.feedback: This is camelCase, not PascalCase.

C.text: ShoppingCart
C.feedback: Correct! React component names are typically written in PascalCase.

D.text: shopping-cart
D.feedback: This is kebab-case, not PascalCase.

question: What happens if a React component is named 'navbar' instead of 'Navbar'?

A.text: React treats it as an HTML element instead of a component
A.feedback: Correct! Lowercase names are treated as HTML elements in JSX.

B.feedback: React does not automatically rename components.

C.text: The component will render faster
C.feedback: Naming does not affect rendering speed.

D.text: Nothing changes because capitalization does not matter
D.feedback: Capitalization matters in React because it determines whether something is treated as a component or an HTML element.

question: Which JSX line correctly renders a component named ProfileCard?

A.text: <profileCard />
A.feedback: Lowercase names are treated as HTML elements, not React components.

B.feedback: Correct! React components should be written in PascalCase and rendered using JSX syntax.

C.text: <profile-card />
C.feedback: This uses kebab-case and would be treated as a custom HTML tag.

D.text: <PROFILECARD />
D.feedback: This is not PascalCase and does not follow React naming conventions.

question: What must a React component return in order to render correctly?

A.text: Only a string
A.feedback: React components are not limited to strings. They must return valid JSX or null.

B.feedback: Correct! A React component must return valid JSX to render UI, or null if it should render nothing.

C.text: A JavaScript variable
C.feedback: A variable can be used inside JSX, but the component itself must return valid JSX or null.

D.text: Only HTML elements
D.feedback: React components return JSX, which resembles HTML but is JavaScript syntax.

question: What happens if a React component does not return anything?

A.text: The component will not work correctly because React expects JSX or null to be returned
A.feedback: Correct! React components must return valid JSX or null. Forgetting to return something will cause problems.

B.text: React automatically returns an empty div
B.feedback: React does not automatically add a div for you. Components must explicitly return JSX or null.

C.text: The component will still render normally
C.feedback: A component that returns nothing will not render correctly because React expects JSX or null.

D.text: React converts the function into JSX automatically
D.feedback: React does not automatically generate JSX for a component. The function must explicitly return it.

question: When would a React component return null?

A.text: When the component wants to render nothing
A.feedback: Correct! Returning null tells React to render nothing for that component.

B.feedback: Returning null is intentional and does not mean the component crashed.

C.text: Whenever the component contains state
C.feedback: Components using state still return JSX. Returning null is only used when nothing should be rendered.

D.text: Whenever a component uses an event handler
D.feedback: Event handlers do not require returning null. Components still return JSX unless nothing should render.

question: Which of the following is a valid return value for a React component?

A.text: return 42;
A.feedback: React components should return valid JSX or null, not arbitrary values like numbers.

B.feedback: Correct! Returning null is valid and tells React to render nothing.

C.text: return updatePage();
C.feedback: Calling a function is not necessarily valid unless it returns JSX. Components themselves should return JSX or null.

D.text: return setCount(1);
D.feedback: State setters do not return JSX. Components must return valid JSX or null.

question: Why is returning JSX important in a React component?

A.text: Because JSX tells React what should appear on the screen
A.feedback: Correct! JSX describes what React should render in the UI.

B.text: Because JSX makes components run faster
B.feedback: JSX is not primarily about performance. It describes what UI should be rendered.

C.text: Because React components cannot contain JavaScript
C.feedback: React components are JavaScript functions and can absolutely contain JavaScript.

D.text: Because JSX automatically updates state
D.feedback: JSX does not update state. State updates happen through hooks like useState.

question: Which syntax is used to render a React component named Header?

A.text: <Header />
A.feedback: Correct! React components are rendered using JSX syntax like <Header />.

B.feedback: Calling a component like a regular function is not the standard way to render it in React. Components should be rendered using JSX syntax.

C.text: {Header}
C.feedback: This only references the component function. It does not render the component.

D.text: <header></header>
D.feedback: This is an HTML element, not a React component. React components begin with a capital letter.

question: Why is <Counter /> used instead of Counter() when rendering a React component?

A.text: Because React components must always be written in lowercase
A.feedback: React components are actually written in PascalCase (capitalized names).

B.text: Because JSX syntax tells React to treat it as a component
B.feedback: Correct! JSX syntax tells React to render the component properly and integrate it into React’s rendering system.

C.text: Because regular functions cannot return JSX
C.feedback: Regular functions can return JSX. React components are functions themselves.

D.text: Because React does not allow functions to be called
D.feedback: React allows functions to be called. The preferred way to render components is through JSX syntax.

question: Which example correctly renders a component named ProfileCard?

A.text: {ProfileCard()}
A.feedback: This directly calls the function instead of rendering it using JSX syntax.

B.feedback: Component names should start with a capital letter in React. Lowercase names are treated as HTML elements.

C.text: <ProfileCard />
C.feedback: Correct! React components are rendered with JSX syntax and PascalCase naming.

D.text: render(ProfileCard)
D.feedback: React components are rendered using JSX syntax, not a render() function like this.

question:
What is wrong with this code?

<HomePage></HomePage>

A.text: Nothing is wrong. This is valid JSX syntax for rendering a component
A.feedback: Correct! <HomePage></HomePage> is valid JSX syntax, though <HomePage /> is more commonly used for components without children.

B.text: Components cannot be rendered with angle brackets
B.feedback: React components are rendered using JSX syntax with angle brackets.

C.text: The component should be wrapped in curly braces
C.feedback: Components are rendered directly in JSX using angle brackets, not wrapped in curly braces.

D.text: Components cannot contain capital letters
D.feedback: React component names should begin with a capital letter.

question: What does JSX syntax like <Navbar /> tell React?

A.text: To create a normal JavaScript variable
A.feedback: JSX syntax is used for rendering UI, not creating variables.

B.feedback: Correct! JSX syntax tells React to render the component.

C.text: To automatically update component state
C.feedback: JSX does not automatically update state. State updates happen through hooks like useState.

D.text: To call a browser API
D.feedback: JSX syntax is for rendering UI, not browser APIs.

question: Why is the useState hook necessary in a React component?

A.text: It lets JavaScript variables be used inside JSX
A.feedback: useState is not required just to display values in JSX. Regular variables can also be displayed, but useState is needed when the value changes and the UI should update.

B.text: It allows React to track values and re-render the component when those values change
B.feedback: Correct! useState allows React to track state values and re-render the component whenever those values change.

C.text: It automatically updates all HTML elements in the component
C.feedback: useState does not directly update HTML elements. Instead, it tells React that state has changed so React can re-render the component.

D.text: It is required whenever a component uses a button
D.feedback: Buttons do not require useState. useState is only needed when a component must track changing values and update the UI.

question: What problem does useState solve in a React component?

A.text: It allows functions to return JSX
A.feedback: Components can return JSX without useState. The purpose of useState is to manage changing values that should persist between renders.

B.feedback: useState is not used to make variables update faster. It is used so React can track state changes and re-render the component when needed.

C.text: It allows a component to remember changing values between renders
C.feedback: Correct! useState allows a component to preserve values between renders and update the UI when those values change.

D.text: It allows components to call other components
D.feedback: Components can render other components without useState. The purpose of useState is to store values that can change over time.

question: When should you use the useState hook in a React component?

A.text: Whenever a component contains JSX
A.feedback: A component can contain JSX without using useState. useState is only needed when the component needs to track changing values.

B.text: Whenever a component contains a function
B.feedback: Components can contain functions without using useState. useState is only necessary when managing changing state.

C.text: Whenever you create a variable inside a component
C.feedback: Regular variables can still be used inside components. useState is specifically for values that should trigger a re-render when updated.

D.text: When a value changes and the UI should update
D.feedback: Correct! useState should be used when a value changes over time and those changes should be reflected in the UI.

question: Why would a regular variable not work well for updating information on a webpage in React?

A.text: Because regular variables are slower than state variables
A.feedback: The issue is not performance. React simply does not re-render when a normal variable changes.

B.text: Because changing a regular variable will not automatically trigger a re-render
B.feedback: Correct! React does not track regular variables, so changing them will not automatically update what appears on the screen.

C.text: Because React components cannot use regular variables
C.feedback: React components can absolutely use regular variables. The issue is that changing them does not trigger a re-render.

D.text: Because regular variables can only store numbers
D.feedback: Regular variables can store any type of data. The problem is that React does not track changes to them for rendering.

question: A student wants to update text shown on a webpage after clicking a button. Why should they use useState?

A.text: Because buttons only work when useState is used
A.feedback: Buttons can work perfectly fine without useState. useState is needed when clicking the button changes displayed information.

B.text: Because React does not allow variables inside components
B.feedback: React components can use variables. However, normal variables do not trigger re-renders when changed.

C.text: Because useState allows React to update the displayed information when the value changes
C.feedback: Correct! useState tells React when state changes so the component can re-render and display updated information.

D.text: Because useState makes button clicks happen faster
D.feedback: useState is not about performance. It is used to track changing values and update the UI.
